title: Cost And Latency Budget Packet
summary: Run data, finance guidance, and user feedback about trace depth, retries, and demo responsiveness.

sources.title: Run telemetry
sources.body:
The candidate prompt reduced unsupported claims but increased median token usage by 11%. Latency improved by 6% because fewer generations needed manual reruns, even though each retained trace is longer.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the cost latency tradeoff case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Candidate cost increased but stays within the 1.15x guardrail. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Latency improved because fewer manual reruns were needed.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not optimize cost by deleting traces reviewers need for grounding failures. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Finance guidance
sources.body:
Finance approved up to a 1.15x cost ratio for demo-quality eval runs if the report shows why extra tokens reduce review time. Anything above that threshold needs a smaller case batch or stricter retry cap.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the cost latency tradeoff case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Latency improved because fewer manual reruns were needed. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Long traces need claim-level summaries, not raw payloads alone.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not ignore the 1.15x cost threshold. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Reviewer feedback
sources.body:
Reviewers said long traces are useful only when they are summarized next to the claim that failed. Raw provider payloads alone slowed review because people had to search for the relevant evidence.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the cost latency tradeoff case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Long traces need claim-level summaries, not raw payloads alone. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Single-briefing UX should stay under 10 seconds while eval runs show progress.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not optimize cost by deleting traces reviewers need for grounding failures. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Demo rehearsal
sources.body:
The live demo felt responsive when single briefings finished under 10 seconds and eval progress showed the current case. Stakeholders were tolerant of longer full runs if progress and artifact paths updated continuously.

Briefing relevance: This document is part of the authored synthetic source packet for the cost latency tradeoff case. The strongest signal for the requested briefing is: Single-briefing UX should stay under 10 seconds while eval runs show progress. A secondary signal, which may or may not belong in the final recommendation depending on the user request, is: Candidate cost increased but stays within the 1.15x guardrail.

Decision boundary: A good briefing should cite this document only for claims it directly supports. The main trap to avoid is: Do not ignore the 1.15x cost threshold. If this document is cited for a broader rollout, approval, or safety claim, the evaluator should check whether the body text actually contains that gate.

sources.title: Reviewer calibration document
sources.body:
Reviewers use this calibration document to decide whether the briefing selected the right evidence from the packet. The expected coverage points for this case are: Candidate cost increased but stays within the 1.15x guardrail. Latency improved because fewer manual reruns were needed. Long traces need claim-level summaries, not raw payloads alone. Single-briefing UX should stay under 10 seconds while eval runs show progress.

The calibration note is intentionally not a model answer. It gives the evaluator enough context to distinguish direct support from plausible synthesis. A strong briefing should still rely on the source-specific operational notes above when making claims, and it should use this document only to understand how the packet will be judged.

The repeated failure modes under review are cost-guardrail, trace-usability, latency. When a generated briefing cites sources broadly without connecting the claim to a concrete caveat, gate, or decision boundary, reviewers should mark the citation as weak even if the source is topically related.

sources.title: Risk and distractor document
sources.body:
This document records traps that make the packet realistic for eval work. Known traps include: Do not optimize cost by deleting traces reviewers need for grounding failures. Do not ignore the 1.15x cost threshold.

Several details in the packet are deliberately adjacent to the user request but not sufficient for a shipping recommendation. For example, a metric improvement should not automatically become a rollout recommendation, a faster path should not automatically become the default path, and a draft or generated recommendation should not be treated as an approved action.

The intended task is: Recommend how much tracing and retry behavior to keep for the next demo run. A good answer should prioritize the evidence that changes the recommendation, identify caveats that block overconfident claims, and leave open questions where the packet does not contain enough information.
